item.title: The Ash Keepers — Season 22
item.description: Avatar set created for the W3Champions (W3C) and Back2Warcraft (B2W) esports community, celebrating Season 22 of competitive Warcraft 3. Each design captures the personality of the players and the visual language of the community.

item.title: The Ash Keepers of Season 22 — B2W × W3C Avatars
item.description:
Season 22 of the W3Champions ladder brought another opportunity to create custom avatars for the Back2Warcraft esports community. These pieces are a small but meaningful part of the competitive scene — players earn or receive them as recognition within the community.

Each avatar in the Ash Keepers set was designed to carry a distinct identity while fitting cohesively into the visual language of the W3C ecosystem. The challenge is always to make something that feels personal to the individual while also reading clearly at small display sizes.

item.title: Commission: Spellbringers Key Art
item.description: Key art commissioned for Spellbringers, a published Warcraft 3 mod in the strategy genre. The piece needed to convey the scope and visual identity of the game while working within the aesthetic expectations of the WC3 modding community.

item.title: Commission: Spellbringers Key Art
item.description:
Spellbringers is a published Warcraft 3 mod in the strategy genre with an active player base. The commission was for key art — the kind of image that represents the game as a whole, used for promotion, cover imagery, and community presence.

Key art is a different discipline than character design or illustration. It has to communicate the genre, tone, and stakes of the game at a glance. For a strategy mod rooted in the Warcraft 3 community, there’s a visual vocabulary players already know — and the piece has to feel native to that space while also standing on its own.

item.title: NDLZ – The Sixth Prime
item.description:
NDLZ – The Sixth Prime was our entry for JS13K Games 2022 — a jam where the entire game, including assets, must fit within 13 kilobytes of JavaScript.

The constraint is brutal and clarifying at the same time. Every byte matters. You can’t rely on frameworks or asset pipelines. You have to think in fundamentals.

We entered with a clear goal: not just to submit something, but to use the jam as a vehicle to grow. The collaboration was as much about strengthening our working relationship and shared understanding as it was about the final product. Building something small and complete together does more for a team than months of loose planning.

item.title: NDLZ – The Sixth Prime
item.description:
NDLZ – The Sixth Prime is a game built for JS13K Games 2022, a competition where the entire game — code, assets, and all — must fit within 13 kilobytes.

The Constraint

13KB forces you to be deliberate about everything. No frameworks. No asset pipelines. Pure JavaScript, hand-optimized. The constraint is the point: it strips game development down to fundamentals and forces creative problem-solving at every level.

The Collaboration

The project was built collaboratively with the explicit goal of using the jam as a vehicle for growth. We wanted to sharpen skills, deepen our understanding of game systems, and strengthen the working relationship — not just ship something.

item.title: Commission: Gentle Avatar
item.description: A commissioned avatar for a Warcraft 3 Champions player. The brief called for something that reflected the player’s personality — approachable and clean, with strong character presence suited to the WC3 competitive scene.

item.title: Commission: Gentle Avatar
item.description:
Avatar commissions for W3Champions players are always personal — the avatar represents someone in a competitive community they care about. The brief for this one was clear: approachable, friendly, with strong visual presence even at small sizes.

Avatar design lives at the intersection of portraiture and icon design. It needs to work at 64×64 pixels but also hold up when displayed larger on stream or profile pages. Every element has to earn its place.

item.title: UF: VERG – Castleroid: 02
item.description:
The Castleroid exercise is exactly what it sounds like — a deliberate study in the tile-based graphic language of classic Castlevania and Metroidvania games.

The goal isn’t to clone anything. It’s to deeply understand how those games construct a sense of place with limited pixels. Every tile is a decision about value, silhouette, and repetition. Doing this work by hand — without shortcuts — teaches things that reading about pixel art never can.

item.title: SMS Graphics — Mercer Creek Church
item.description: A series of social media notification graphics produced for Mercer Creek Church. The project was an opportunity to learn from experienced designers working within consistent content cycles — a valuable experience in adapting to an established visual style and client workflow.

item.title: SMS Graphics — Mercer Creek Church
item.description:
This project was an opportunity to grow into a graphic style I wasn’t already comfortable with. Mercer Creek Church had a clear visual identity and a consistent content cycle — which meant I was learning to adapt to someone else’s established system, not just executing my own ideas.

That’s a different skill. When you work within an existing design language, you have to study it carefully: the color palette, the typefaces, the spacing, the emotional register. Then you have to produce work that feels native to it, not like something grafted on from outside.

item.title: Cheerful — Reynor
item.description: A tribute piece for Reynor (Riccardo Romiti), the Italian StarCraft 2 professional player and world champion. Created to celebrate his rise in the competitive scene.

item.title: StarCraft 2 Fan Art
item.description: Fan art born out of late-night sessions playing StarCraft 2 with friends. The pieces range from quick pencil sketches to more finished digital illustrations inspired by the game’s races and lore.

item.title: EZi BackGrounds
item.description:
EZi BackGrounds is a collection of desktop wallpapers designed around one principle: be gentle on the eyes.

The Problem

Most wallpapers optimize for visual impact — high contrast, saturated colors, dramatic compositions. That’s fine for first impressions, but if you’re staring at your desktop for eight-plus hours a day, it becomes a source of fatigue. Blue-heavy palettes disrupt sleep. High contrast causes visual strain. For people with light sensitivity or migraines, it’s actively harmful.

item.title: Honey Badger Decal
item.description: An original honey badger character design built around the motto “NEVER QUIT!” — produced as a decal and later adapted into a desktop wallpaper.

item.title: Honey Badger Decal Wallpaper
item.description:
The honey badger is a fitting mascot for a “Never Quit!” motto — the animal is famously relentless. This design started as a decal concept and expanded into a desktop wallpaper because the composition held up at larger sizes.

Original character, original design. No references beyond the animal itself and a strong sense of what the motto should feel like visually.

The wallpaper is available to download — click the title and right-click to save the full-size PNG.

item.title: Ikora Rey of Light
item.description: A portrait study of Ikora Rey, the Warlock Vanguard from Destiny. Focused on capturing her strength and composure through warm lighting and a painterly finish.

item.title: Bring the Light
item.description: A fan illustration rooted in the Destiny universe. The piece focuses on the contrast between light and void — a theme central to Guardian mythology.

item.title: Destiny Avatar: E4 KEYEZ
item.description: A custom-rendered PlayStation Network profile avatar built around my Destiny character — E4 KEYEZ. Blends in-game aesthetics with a hand-crafted illustration approach.

item.title: Destiny Avatar: E4 KEYEZ
item.description:
E4 KEYEZ is my Destiny character — the Guardian I’ve sunk hundreds of hours into. This piece was a personal project: building a proper avatar for the PlayStation Network profile using the character as a foundation but pushing beyond what the game renders.

The challenge was staying true to the in-game aesthetic while adding enough hand-crafted illustration to make it feel like art rather than a screenshot. The Destiny visual language is dense — lots of intricate armor detail, dramatic lighting, and a specific color palette per class.

item.title: Ghost Reign
item.description: An entry submitted to the official StarCraft Remastered portrait contest. The piece depicts the Ghost unit in a painterly style intended to complement the remastered visual direction.

item.title: Ghost Reign — StarCraft Remastered Portrait Contest
item.description:
When Blizzard opened submissions for the StarCraft Remastered portrait contest, I knew I had to enter. StarCraft has been a constant presence in my creative life — through the esports communities I’ve worked with, the fan art I’ve made, and the friendships the game has facilitated.

Ghost Reign features the Ghost unit rendered in a painterly style intended to complement the remastered visual direction. The Ghost is a compelling subject — silent, precise, operating in the space between human and weapon.

item.title: Cheermotes for BaseTrade TV
item.description:
BaseTrade TV is a StarCraft 2 broadcast organization with a strong community presence on Twitch. Cheermotes are animated emotes tied to the Bits cheer system — small, expressive animations that play when viewers donate.

The brief called for something that bridged the pixel art aesthetic with modern emote readability. The result was a hybrid style: the chunky geometry and limited palette of pixel art, but with enough polish to work at Twitch emote sizes.

item.title: WIP — Mecromage Animation Demo
item.description:
Mecromage has been in development for longer than I care to admit. It started as a concept in 2013 and has gone through more iterations than I can count — which is both a testament to stubbornness and a record of how much my craft has grown.

The animation demo collects a set of sprite-based assets built in Adobe Animate CC: walk cycles, idle states, and combat motions. These aren’t the final designs, but they represent a significant milestone in moving from concept to something that actually moves.

item.title: UF: VERG – Castleroid
item.description:
The Castleroid project is a deliberate study in retro tile-based graphics. The goal: understand the visual language of classic Castlevania at a deep level by recreating and then extending it.

Why This Exercise

The Castlevania series solved a hard problem — how to build a sense of place, dread, and wonder from a 16×16 tile grid with a handful of colors. Understanding how that works requires doing it, not just looking at it.

item.title: Mecromage
item.description:
Mecromage is a side-scrolling Metroidvania that has been in development, on and off, since 2013. It’s the longest-running creative project in my portfolio — and the one I’m most committed to finishing.

Concept

The name blends “Mechromancer” and “Mage” — the game sits at the intersection of mechanical and arcane. The protagonist navigates an interconnected world that rewards exploration and punishes complacency.

The art direction balances dark fantasy with a mechanical edge: heavy armor, industrial environments, creatures that blur the line between built and born.

item.title: Unchosen Paths
item.description:
Unchosen Paths was an indie game studio I co-founded in 2009 with a guitarist known for Castlevania remixes. The name reflects the creative philosophy: taking roads that aren’t mapped out yet.

My Role

I served as producer, artist, game director, and software designer. That range wasn’t by choice so much as by necessity — small studios require everyone to do everything. But it was the best possible education in what it actually takes to make a game.

item.title: About
item.description:
I’m Joshua Keyes — a freelance artist working in digital illustration, animation, logo design, game design, and web design. I grew up in Washington State and served in the United States Marine Corps, completing two deployments to Iraq as an infantryman.

After the Corps, I studied animation and transfer studies at Mount San Antonio College in California, then built a career at the intersection of visual design and the gaming community.
